title: Paper clay relief portrait tile
subtitle: A 15 cm square wall tile with a face modelled in low relief from paper clay
excerpt: A 15 cm square wall tile with a stylised face modelled in low relief on the surface. Paper clay is the material of choice here: the cellulose fibres support the built-up relief shapes and reduce cracking around the eyes, nose, and mouth as the tile dries. No kiln required. The finished tile is wall-mounted indoors.
sourceNotes: Relief portrait tiles are documented across medieval European and Renaissance traditions. V&A open-access collection for relief-tile reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Clay with cellulose fibres mixed in, which strengthens joins and thin sections and reduces cracking. Particularly useful for sculptural work with built-up relief.
Sculpted decoration that stands slightly proud of the background surface, typically by 3 to 10 mm. Distinct from high relief, where forms project further from the surface.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with slurry before pressing together. The standard join for adding relief shapes onto a base slab.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and carve cleanly.
Air-dried but unfired. For paper clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: A 15 cm square wall tile with a stylised face modelled in
low relief
on the surface. The base is a flat slab; the brow, nose, cheeks, and chin are built up from added pieces of clay scored and slipped onto the surface.
Paper clay
is the right material because the cellulose fibres bind the added pieces to the slab and reduce cracking around the modelled features. No kiln required. The finished tile is wall-mounted indoors.
Preparation

Wedge the paper clay for two minutes on the board. Paper clay needs gentler wedging than plain clay; over-wedging breaks the cellulose fibres and loses some of their strengthening effect. Reserve 100 g for the relief features. The remaining 300 g forms the base slab.
Rolling the base

Pat the 300 g into a thick rectangle. Lay the guide rails on either side and roll to an even 8 mm thickness. The base of a relief tile is built thicker than a plain slab to support the added weight of the modelled features and to resist warping as the relief dries faster than the base. Let the slab rest 5 minutes.
Lay the 15 cm card template on the slab and cut around it with the fettling knife held vertical. Lift the cut square onto a fresh board.
Modelling the face

The face is built from a small number of added shapes rather than carved into the slab. This is the strongest construction in paper clay and the most forgiving for a beginner in relief modelling. The features described here are stylised; aim for clean shapes that read well from a distance rather than realism.
1. Lay out the face oval
Lightly score an oval outline onto the slab with the needle tool: about 9 cm tall and 7 cm wide, centred in the square. The scored line is a guide; the relief sits inside it.
2. Add the brow ridge and nose

Roll a small coil of paper clay about 6 mm thick and shape it into a single arched ridge that runs across the upper third of the oval, dropping down the centre to form the nose. Score the base slab where the ridge will sit and brush with slurry. Press the ridge on with a finger, smoothing the lower edge into the slab so it merges with the surface. The ridge should stand 5 mm proud of the slab at the brow and 8 mm proud at the tip of the nose.
3. Add the cheeks
Roll two small balls of paper clay, each about 10 mm across. Flatten slightly into discs.
Score and slip

the slab where each cheek will sit, just below and outside the brow line. Press the discs on and blend the outer edge of each cheek into the slab with a fingertip or the rib so the cheek rises smoothly out of the surface rather than sitting on it like a button.
4. Add the chin and mouth

Add a small rounded triangle of clay for the chin at the lower end of the oval, blending the upper edges into the slab. The mouth is a single horizontal score with the needle tool above the chin; or add a small ridge of clay shaped into upper and lower lips for a more pronounced mouth.
5. Mark the eyes

Press two small almond-shaped indentations into the slab under the brow ridge with the back of a teaspoon or a small modelling tool. Indenting (rather than adding) keeps the eyes recessed, which reads as eyes against the raised brow and cheeks. A single small pressed dot at the centre of each indent suggests a pupil.
6. Hair or surround (optional)

Above the brow, add a flowing curve of hair, a halo, or a plain frame. Roll a 6 mm coil and lay it in a curve from temple to temple over the top of the head, or press a series of finger-pressed indentations into the slab around the face. Score and slip any added pieces as before.
Pierce the hanging hole

Press a drinking straw or a piping nozzle through the tile 15 mm down from the top edge, centred. Twist gently as it comes back out. This gives an alternative hanging point if a glued D-ring is not preferred. Pierce while the slab is still soft.
Drying

Relief tiles warp as they dry because the relief side dries faster than the back. The fix is slow drying and weighting the tile flat. Set the tile face-up on the board. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours. Once the surface feels firm to the touch, place a clean piece of card across the relief and a light board or tile on top to weight it flat for the next 24 hours. Remove the weighting once the tile reaches
leather-hard
. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes four to seven days at this thickness.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand the edges of the tile and the back with 220-grit sandpaper. Avoid sanding the relief; the modelled forms are best left with the surface they had when modelled. Brush off the dust. Paint with acrylics: a single colour wash over the whole tile catches the relief and reads strongly as a face, or paint the features in contrasting colours. Seal with two coats of acrylic varnish. If using a glued D-ring rather than the pierced hole, attach it to the back of the bone-dry tile with epoxy or strong PVA before sealing.
Care

Paper clay is not waterproof. Hang the tile indoors, away from damp walls (a bathroom is not the right place). Dust with a soft cloth. Re-coat with a thin layer of varnish every few years if the surface dulls.
Variations
Model a leaf or fern relief instead of a face. The same add-and-blend method gives a botanical wall tile in the same format.
Cut a round 15 cm tile instead of square for a frame-like presentation.
Make a matched set of three tiles in different colour finishes for a small wall grouping.

items.symptom: Tile warps backwards (curves toward the back)
items.cause: Relief side dried faster than the back
items.fix: Weight the tile flat with a board across the relief for the first 24 hours once the surface is firm; press the warp gently back while still leather-hard

items.symptom: Added relief features lift off the slab as the tile dries
items.cause: Score-and-slip was inadequate, or plain clay was used instead of paper clay
items.fix: Always use paper clay for built-up relief; score the slab and the underside of each added piece deeply, and brush a thick slurry between

items.symptom: Cracks run around the edges of added features
items.cause: Added pieces dried faster than the base slab
items.fix: Cover the whole tile with plastic for the first 24 hours; press fresh paper clay slurry into any small cracks before they widen

items.symptom: Hanging hole tears the rim after firing the tile is hung
items.cause: Hole was too small or pierced too close to the edge
items.fix: Pierce a 4 mm hole at least 15 mm down from the top edge; use a glued D-ring for heavier tiles or for long-term hanging
